title: Pre-LA piece-to-camera — 'Nine days. LA. Five questions.'
coreArgument: Tim coming to LA with questions, not a pitch deck, signals operator-confidence and surfaces inbound from people who want to meet — and being on camera (not just writing) is what a vertical-native operator looks like in 2026.
whyNow: LA trip Jun 1–4 is 9 days out. The window to set the narrative BEFORE Tim lands is closing. Pre-trip piece-to-camera turns a private calendar item into a public agenda before he walks into a single meeting.
sourceEvidence: Confirmed LA meetings trip Jun 1–4 (data/state/speaking.json). Tim's weekly focus: 'Build LA trip momentum.' knowledge/VOICE.md → On-camera voice rules.
timPOV: Hollywood keeps asking the wrong question. The interesting question isn't 'is vertical real?' — that was 2024. The interesting question is what gets built on top of it.
colRelevance: Frames Tim as the operator Hollywood is coming to, not the operator selling Hollywood. Surfaces inbound for COL's Microdrama-in-a-Box B2B narrative ahead of meetings. Visual native = COL native.
supportingPoints: **Open (0–3 sec):** Tim outside — Singapore daylight, not at a desk. Phone at chest height. Camera straight on.
**Beat 1 (3–10 sec):** 'Nine days. LA. Five questions.' Hold beat. No music.

**Beat 2 (10–35 sec):** Three of the five questions on camera, fast — '(1) Which production execs are quietly piloting vertical? (2) Which agencies have a real creator-vertical strategy? (3) Which brand CMOs are buying vertical drama as a media line item, not a stunt?' Two more in the caption.
**Beat 3 (35–50 sec):** 'I'm not coming to LA to sell anything. I'm coming to find out what people are actually doing when nobody's posting about it.'
**Close (50–60 sec):** 'I'll tell you what I find.' Forward line, no sign-off. Cut.
**Caption (~90 words):** Restate the hook, list ALL five questions, end with 'Who should I be asking?' — generates inbound.
risk: Tim sounding like he's auditioning Hollywood. Mitigation: shot OUTSIDE a corporate setting (no COL-branded backdrop), no suit jacket, energy not gravitas. Watch the cuts for swagger.
body:
[SHOOT INSTRUCTIONS]
Location: Singapore daylight, somewhere with depth in the background — not a desk, not a logo wall. A street, a hotel lobby, the FlareFlow studio entrance.
Wardrobe: white tee or open-collar shirt. NO suit jacket. NO tie.
Phone at chest height, vertical, native camera app. Single take. Allow 3–4 attempts.
Audio: phone mic is fine. If outdoors, find a wall to break wind noise.

[ON-CAMERA SCRIPT — ~60 seconds total]

(Direct, energy, half a beat more confident than written-Tim. No 'hey everyone'. No 'so'.)

Nine days. LA. Five questions.

(Beat.)

One. Which production execs are quietly piloting vertical right now — and which are still pretending not to.

Two. Which agencies have a real creator-vertical strategy that isn't just "we'll repackage TikTok talent."

Three. Which brand CMOs are buying vertical drama as a media line item — not a one-off stunt.

(Two more in the caption.)

I'm not coming to LA to sell anything. I'm coming to find out what people are actually doing when nobody's posting about it.

I'll tell you what I find.

[CAPTION TEXT — for LinkedIn / X post body]

Nine days. LA. Five questions I'm going to ask everyone.

1. Which production execs are quietly piloting vertical right now — and which are still pretending not to?

2. Which agencies have a real creator-vertical strategy, not just "we'll repackage TikTok talent"?

3. Which brand CMOs are buying vertical drama as a media line item — not a one-off stunt?

4. Which platforms are licensing vertical IP vs commissioning it?

5. What does the next CAA / WME signing in vertical look like — and from where?

Who should I be asking?
rationale: Hook in 5 words — passes the VOICE.md on-camera test. Outdoor, phone, no jacket — passes the 'not a corporate piece' test. Specific questions with named institutions (CAA, WME, brand CMOs) — passes the 'no fluff' test. Forward close ('I'll tell you what I find') sets up the LA daily vlog series — building cadence. Generates inbound from the caption's closing question. This replaces last week's written version of the same idea.

title: Day 1 LA travel vlog — beat sheet (record Jun 1)
hook: Day one. LA. Here's the list.
coreArgument: Daily ~60-second packages from LA build cadence + agenda — turn a four-day private trip into four public artefacts that compound across the rest of the LA-week posts.
whyNow: Tim lands LA Jun 1. Day-1 beat needs to be shot ON Jun 1 — not from notes after the fact. The cadence is the value; one daily post Jun 1–Jun 4 is the deliverable.
sourceEvidence: Confirmed LA trip Jun 1–4 (data/state/speaking.json). knowledge/OPPORTUNITY_RADAR.md → Category 21 Visual content / travel vlog beats. knowledge/VOICE.md → 'Always shoot when travelling.'
timPOV: Most exec travel content is either 'arrived in city, excited to meet team' (corporate, dead) or 'look at the view from my hotel' (extractive, dead). The version that lands: name the people, name the questions, name the day. Specific beats specificity.
colRelevance: Builds Tim's recognisability in LA / Hollywood B-roll. Establishes 'Tim shows up' as a brand-level expectation — recurrence is part of mogul-construction.
supportingPoints: **Open (0–4 sec):** Walking shot. LAX exit OR Sunset Blvd OR Studio gates. 'Day one. LA. Here's the list.' Hold.
**Beat 1 (4–20 sec):** Name 3 meetings publicly (only if confirmed + non-confidential). Format: 'Today: [name / role], [name / role], [name / role].' Direct to camera. Walking.
**Beat 2 (20–40 sec):** Frame for the day. 'The question I'm bringing: [one specific question — e.g. "What does a brand-CMO want from vertical that they're not getting?"].'
**Beat 3 (40–55 sec):** One overheard detail / first impression. 'Already noticed: [thing]. We'll see if that holds.' (No spin, just observation.)
**Close (55–60 sec):** 'Tomorrow.' Cut.
**Caption (~50 words):** Recap the question. Tag the people if their names were used on camera (with their permission). End with 'Day 2 tomorrow.'
**Same beat sheet for Days 2, 3, 4** — substitute meetings + question + observation. Tim's job is to film 60 seconds per day, not to script a series. Stuart caption-edits + posts.
risk: Naming meetings that haven't given permission to be named publicly. Mitigation: ONLY name people Stuart has cleared. Default to 'studio exec' / 'agency partner' if no clearance. Risk #2: Tim's energy flags by Day 3. Mitigation: shoot Day 3 in the morning, not after a 9pm dinner.
body:
[BEAT SHEET — not a script. Tim improvises within the structure each day.]

DAY 1 — JUN 1 (record between landing + first meeting)

Location options (pick what feels native): LAX exit (the wheelchair luggage moment), arriving at the hotel, Sunset Blvd walking shot, studio lot gates (if visiting one).

Opening: 'Day one. LA. Here's the list.'

Meetings to name (Stuart confirms permission for each name on the morning of Jun 1):
  • Meeting 1 — [name / role] (or fallback: 'studio exec at [studio]')
  • Meeting 2 — [name / role] (or fallback: 'agency partner')
  • Meeting 3 — [name / role] (or fallback: 'investor — consumer + China thesis')

Question for the day (Tim picks ONE the night before):
  • Option A: 'What does a brand CMO want from vertical that they're not getting?'
  • Option B: 'Which agency has a vertical-native creator desk first — and what would they want from us?'
  • Option C: 'What's the moment Hollywood actually says yes to vertical at scale?'

Overheard / first impression:
  • Whatever lands. Authentic > clever.

Close: 'Tomorrow.' One word. Cut.

[DAY 2 — JUN 2]
Same structure. New meetings. New question (don't repeat).
Location: morning coffee, between two meetings, Beverly Hills sidewalk — wherever.

[DAY 3 — JUN 3]
Same structure. By now Tim should be naming patterns ('Three people have asked me the same question. That tells me...'). Higher value than Day 1.

[DAY 4 — JUN 4]
Final day = recap variant. 'Four days, fifteen meetings, here's what changed my mind.' Slightly longer (~75 sec OK). This is the post that gets reposted — make it the sharpest.

[CAPTION TEMPLATE — same for each day]

Day [N] in LA. Today's question: [the question]. Met: [up to 3 names if cleared].

[One-line observation, copied from the video close.]

Day [N+1] tomorrow.

[POSTING CADENCE]
Daily, between 6pm–9pm LA time (which is the LinkedIn-APAC-morning sweet spot for Tim's home audience + still primetime in US). Stuart edits captions + posts; Tim's only job is to film 60 seconds per day.
rationale: Beat sheet, not a script — Tim improvises within structure, which is more native to vertical-creator energy. Forces ONE question per day = surfaces the LA conversation as it unfolds. Daily cadence = compounding visibility, not one-off. Caption template + clearance protocol = removes Stuart-side friction during the actual trip. Day 4 designed as the share-able recap. This is the kind of asset that makes Tim recognisable in Hollywood B-roll — Category 21 in practice.

title: The Vertical View — issue #1 launch announcement
hook: Six months of writing this in my head. Time to publish it.
coreArgument: Vertical content needs a serious, operator-led publication that treats it as a real category — not a TikTok think-piece. Tim is the person to launch it, because he's the only English-language operator running it at scale.
whyNow: Weekly focus explicitly lists 'Launch Vertical View newsletter'. Quarter goal: cement Vertical 2.0 framing in press. Owning a publication is how you stop being a subject of coverage and start being the source. (Parked from this week's 'Act on this week' surface to respect the 3-item time budget — but kept in drafts for next-week consideration.)
sourceEvidence: Quarter goals in data/state/settings.json. Tim's coverage in TVBIZZ, ContentAsia, SCMP, Vitrina LeaderSpeak (data/state/press.json) demonstrates ongoing trade-press demand.
timPOV: Most writing about microdrama is either chart-of-the-week or 'is this real?' takes. Neither is useful to anyone building. The Vertical View will be the version I'd want to read on a Sunday morning.
colRelevance: Direct owned-media channel for COL signals — investor narrative, partnership announcements, hiring, market data. Removes COL's dependence on inbound trade coverage for narrative control.
supportingPoints: Issue cadence: every other Sunday. Operator depth, not news rehash.
Coverage scope: vertical drama, mobile-first storytelling, brand entry, Asia-to-global content flow, AI in production.
Format: 1 long-read + 1 numbered list + 3 things to read this week. No fluff.
Audience target: people who run things — production, distribution, brand, investment. Not 'creators' as a generic bucket.
First-issue topic: why the Australian payment rate (20%) is the most underread data point in entertainment right now.
risk: Newsletter starts strong then dies. Mitigation: pre-write three issues before launch, schedule a fourth, hand cadence to Stuart for accountability.
body:
Six months ago I started writing one of these in my head every Sunday morning.

Today I'm publishing it.

It's called The Vertical View. Every other Sunday. One long-read, one numbered list, three things to read this week. Operator depth, not news rehash.

Why now: the industry has hit the point where there are more think-pieces than people who can tell you what a 77-minute session looks like in the data.

Why me: I've spent the last year as the only English-language exec running global vertical distribution at scale. 1,700+ titles across 10+ markets. 2.4B+ views. I'm not pretending it's easy. I'm saying it deserves serious writing.

Issue #1 next Sunday: why the Australian payment rate at 20% — more than double most developed markets — is the most underread data point in entertainment right now.

Link in comments. Subscribe if you want this in your inbox. Forward to one person who'd find it useful — that's how I'll know if it works.
rationale: Opens personal, lands the public commitment, sets cadence so accountability is built in. Uses Tim's actual numbers (1,700 / 10 / 2.4B / 77 / 20%). One idea per sentence. Ends with a soft ask (forward to one person) — Tim's natural humility frame. Holds until next week per the time-budget cap.

title: Australia at 20% — the underread number
hook: Australia is converting at 20%. Nobody in this industry is talking about it.
coreArgument: Australia's new-user payment rate at ~20% — more than double most developed markets — disproves the lazy assumption that vertical drama is a developing-market phenomenon. Developed markets are converting harder, not softer.
whyNow: Tim revealed this data at Cairns Crocodiles on May 19. The number deserves a wider second life on LinkedIn — first time it's been published in his own voice rather than via panel coverage.
sourceEvidence: Cairns Crocodiles speaking record (data/state/speaking.json). Australia data was disclosed at the 'Maestros of Microdramas' session with Nikyah Hutchings.
timPOV: If you believed the consensus that vertical drama was a Tier 2/3-market phenomenon, the Australia number breaks your model. The lazy framing — 'it works because people in emerging markets have small phones' — collapses when developed-market conversion is higher than emerging-market conversion.
colRelevance: Establishes COL as the operator with the data — the trade outlets and analysts (Maria Rua Aguete, Omdia; Atul Phadnis, Vitrina) cite Tim because he has numbers nobody else has.
supportingPoints: Australia new-user payment rate: ~20%. More than 2x most developed markets.
Counterfactual: if vertical was a developing-market phenomenon, you'd expect inverse — Australia would be a laggard.

Why it's actually true: in developed markets, time-poor consumers value formats that respect their time. 77-minute sessions on FlareFlow prove the demand was always there for short-attention narrative.
Implication: brand spend follows conversion, not impressions. Australia's payment rate is a brand-CMO signal more than a creator signal.
risk: Single-market datapoint gets framed as cherry-picked. Mitigation: name the rate explicitly as a single market, note Tim is asking why — not asserting it generalises.
body:
Australia is converting at 20%.

New-user payment rate. More than double most developed markets.

Nobody in this industry is talking about it. Most takes still assume vertical drama is a developing-market phenomenon — small phones, small budgets, small attention spans.

The Australia number breaks that frame.

If the consensus were right, Australia would be a laggard. It's the opposite. Developed-market viewers — time-poor, format-literate, brand-saturated — are paying *more* readily, not less.

Which means the question stops being "will vertical work in developed markets?" and starts being "what's the brand-CMO going to do about it?"

Because brand spend follows conversion, not impressions.

I'm not saying Australia generalises. I'm saying it's the most underread number in entertainment right now.

Who's seeing similar in another developed market — would love a quiet DM.
rationale: Opens with the headline number. One idea per sentence. Names the consensus, breaks it, reframes the implication for the audience Tim wants to reach (brand CMOs). Closes with a DM ask — Tim's standard relationship-building move. No hashtags, no emoji.

title: Peacock/ReelShort reaction — 'Hollywood just licensed vertical drama.'
coreArgument: Peacock licensing ReelShort content isn't a format validation — it's a supply chain moment: a major US streamer treating a microdrama-native platform as a formal content supplier for the first time.
whyNow: Peacock/ReelShort deal announced May 11, 2026. First major US streamer to license microdrama content natively. This is the exact commercial trajectory Tim has been predicting publicly since MIP London 2026. The window to react with authority is this weekend — before the news cycle moves on.
sourceEvidence: C21Media report: https://www.c21media.net/news/us-streamer-peacock-picks-up-reelshort-package-to-get-into-microdrama-game/ | TechCrunch: https://techcrunch.com/2026/05/11/bravo-is-creating-unscripted-microdramas-for-the-peacock-app/ | Peacock official: https://www.peacocktv.com/blog/microdramas-are-coming-to-peacock-this-summer-details. Date checked: 2026-05-23.
timPOV: This is not 'Netflix but shorter.' Peacock isn't experimenting with vertical format — they're buying a catalogue from a vertical-native platform. That's a supply-chain decision, not a format trial. When Peacock licenses from ReelShort, every other major streamer's head of content starts a conversation internally.
colRelevance: Direct COL/ReelShort commercial validation. Every future B2B pitch to studios and streamers now includes: 'Peacock just did this. You're next.' Raises the floor on every Microdrama-in-a-Box conversation.
supportingPoints: **Open (0–3 sec):** Tim on camera, direct, somewhere with good natural light — not a desk. 'Hollywood just licensed vertical drama.' Beat.

**Beat 1 (3–20 sec):** 'Peacock just picked up a ReelShort catalogue for its mobile app. Not to experiment with — to launch this summer. That's a procurement decision, not a pilot.' Name the thing precisely.
**Beat 2 (20–45 sec):** 'Two years ago people were asking me if this format was real. Last year they were asking if it would cross over. This week they stopped asking.' Hold the weight of that.
**Beat 3 (45–60 sec):** 'The next question isn't whether vertical drama belongs in major streaming. It's who builds the infrastructure that connects the studios to the supply.' One forward line.
**Close (58–63 sec):** 'I'm in LA next week. Guess what we're talking about.' Cut.
**Caption (~80 words):** Name the deal facts — Peacock + ReelShort + summer launch. Add the B2B implication in one sentence. End: 'Nine days to LA. Different conversations now.' No hashtags.
risk: Sounding triumphalist or gloating. Tim's brand is operator confidence, not industry cheerleader. Mitigation: frame the significance for the AUDIENCE (what it means for them), not for Tim personally. 'When Peacock does this, every studio head is now having an internal meeting' — audience-forward, not self-congratulatory.
body:
[SHOOT INSTRUCTIONS]
Location: Anywhere with natural light and depth — a hotel lobby, street, outdoor terrace, studio entrance. NOT a desk, NOT a logo wall.
Wardrobe: Clean shirt, no jacket. Same energy as the other piece-to-camera.
Phone vertical, single take, 3–4 attempts, pick the best one.
Audio: phone mic. Find quiet.

[ON-CAMERA SCRIPT — ~60 seconds]

Hollywood just licensed vertical drama.

(Beat. Don't rush it.)

Peacock just picked up a ReelShort catalogue for its mobile app. Not to run a pilot. To launch this summer. That's a procurement decision.

Two years ago people were asking me if this format was real. Last year: will it cross over. This week they stopped asking.

The next question isn't whether vertical drama belongs in major streaming.

It's who builds the infrastructure that connects the studios to the supply.

(Pause.)

I'm in LA next week. Guess what we're talking about.

[CAPTION TEXT]

Hollywood just licensed vertical drama.

Peacock picked up a ReelShort content package this month — scripted melodrama, romance, fantasy — for its mobile app, launching this summer. First major US streamer to treat a microdrama-native platform as a formal content supplier.

Two years of 'is this real?' questions just got answered.

The next question is infrastructure: who connects the studios to the supply at scale.

Nine days to LA. Different conversations now.
rationale: Hook is 5 words — passes VOICE.md on-camera test. Outdoor, phone, no jacket. The three-beat time progression ('two years / last year / this week') is Tim's natural rhetorical rhythm. Forward close ('I'm in LA next week') sets up the LA trip narrative without explicitly selling. Caption reinforces the supply-chain frame for the LinkedIn audience who watches on mute. This is the highest-leverage single piece Tim can shoot this weekend.

title: COL new hires post — welcoming Jason Ander and Eileen Low
hook: When Paramount and Disney execs join vertical, the conversation changes.
coreArgument: The hiring of Jason Ander (ex-Paramount, Hulu) and Eileen Low (ex-Disney) into COL's team is a signal, not just a hire — the best commercial talent in traditional media is now betting on vertical distribution infrastructure.
whyNow: Deadline and Variety filed the hire stories this week. Tim's LinkedIn post acknowledging the team, written in his specific warm-and-named voice, is expected — and it's an opportunity to frame COL's US expansion as talent-driven, not just capital-driven.
timPOV: I've always said the answer isn't just the format — it's the people building it. Jason comes in knowing what Paramount and Hulu couldn't figure out about short-form. Eileen brings the Disney discipline to Asia partnerships that the market has been missing. We're not building a microdrama app. We're building the global distribution infrastructure for vertical content. You need people who've run things at scale to do that.
colRelevance: Public signal of COL's US team-build. Elevates the B2B narrative from product to institution. Sets up the Microdrama-in-a-Box pitch by showing operator depth behind the product.
supportingPoints: Name both hires specifically and their previous roles — Tim's voice is full of warm name-drops.
Connect to the category frame: 'People who worked at Paramount and Disney choosing vertical isn't charity — it's a signal about where the category is going.'
Include FlareFlow's TikTok revenue stat ($2M Q1) as the commercial proof the team is joining.
One forward line about the LA trip — 'See you in LA next week.'
risk: Sounding like a press release. Mitigation: Tim writes in 'I' voice, names people as people (not 'talent'), shares one specific thing he told each of them when they joined. Draft below uses this approach.
body:
Two hires I'm proud of.

Jason Ander joins COL as Head of US Partnerships. He spent years at Paramount and Hulu figuring out what traditional media couldn't figure out about digital distribution. Now he's here. There's a reason for that.

Eileen Low is our new Head of Partnerships and Sales for Asia. Ex-Disney, ex-Canal+. She understands both the institutional side of media and the operator side. That combination is rare.

I've said it before: the answer isn't just the format, it's the people building it.

When executives who've worked inside the biggest media companies in the world choose to come build vertical distribution infrastructure — that's not a talent decision. That's a thesis.

FlareFlow is already among TikTok's leading microdrama revenue partners globally. 180 originals in 2026. Over 1,700 titles in distribution across more than 10 territories.

We're building rails. And now we have the right people laying them.

See both of you in LA next week.
rationale: Opens with warmth and specificity — Tim's signature register. Names both hires and their previous companies (institutional credibility via association). Includes the $2M TikTok revenue proof point and the 1,700 titles figure. Ends with a forward close that ties to the LA trip without announcing the trip. No hashtags, no 'excited to announce', no 'thrilled to welcome'. One idea per sentence.

title: NEM Dubrovnik travel vlog beat sheet — 'The week vertical drama goes European.' (record Jun 8)
hook: Dubrovnik. The week vertical drama goes European.
coreArgument: NEM Dubrovnik (Jun 8-11) — Tim's panel 'Microdrama: risky business or industry's next guilty pleasure?' with Maria Rua Aguete, Cassandra Yang, and Lukasz Wysocki — is the week to establish Tim's presence in European TV market discourse with daily vlog beats, not just the panel moment.
whyNow: NEM Dubrovnik Jun 8-11 is 16 days out. The panel has the exact right tension ('risky business or guilty pleasure?') against the backdrop of Peacock validation and China crackdowns happening this week. Tim's position is clear: it's neither risky nor guilty — it's a category. Daily vlog beats Jun 8-11 establish a European trade presence before MIPCOM October.
sourceEvidence: Confirmed speaking engagement (data/state/speaking.json seed-nem-dubrovnik-new-europe-market). Panel: 'Microdrama: risky business or industry's next guilty pleasure?' Co-panellists: Maria Rua Aguete (Omdia), Cassandra Yang (RisingJoy), Lukasz Wysocki. Date checked: 2026-05-23.
timPOV: Every time I arrive at a European TV market, someone asks me 'is this just a Chinese phenomenon?' Dubrovnik this year, the answer is definitively no. Peacock just licensed vertical. P&G just produced 50 episodes. The format is global. The business is global. The panel question answers itself.
colRelevance: European TV market exposure ahead of MIPCOM October. NEM Dubrovnik is the corridor where CEE/Eastern European distribution conversations happen — under-tapped market for COL's 1,700+ title catalogue.
supportingPoints: **Open (Day 1, Jun 8, 0–4 sec):** Airport / ferry / hotel arrival shot. 'Dubrovnik. The week vertical drama goes European.' Direct to camera.

**Beat 1 (4–20 sec):** 'I'm here for a panel with Maria Rua Aguete. She has the market sizing. I have the receipts from the field. Together we're going to answer a question the European market's been avoiding.' Don't name the question yet — leave a hook.
**Beat 2 (20–40 sec):** First impression from the room: 'Already in three conversations about [observation]. That's new versus last year.' Authentic field reporting.
**Beat 3 (40–55 sec):** The question for the day — one thing Tim wants to learn from the European side of the room.
**Close (55–62 sec):** 'Panel is Tuesday. I'll take you in.' Forward setup.
**Panel day (Jun 9-10):** Shoot a 30-sec pre-panel piece outside the room — 'Three minutes to go. Here's what I'm going to say.' One sentence, direct to camera. Then recap post-panel.
**Caption (~60 words):** Name Maria, Cassandra, Lukasz on Day 1. Quote the panel question. End: 'Day 2 tomorrow.'
risk: Risk #1: Panel turns into promotional fluff — Tim's 'I do not enjoy fluff' reputation is on the line with European audiences who've seen bad vertical pitches. Mitigation: Tim's opening line on panel should acknowledge the critical frame directly ('risky business? Yes, if you're building it wrong — here's what that looks like.'). Risk #2: Naming panellists without their permission in daily vlog. Mitigation: Stuart clears with Maria / Cassandra ahead of Jun 8.
body:
[BEAT SHEET — NEM Dubrovnik, Jun 8-11. Tim improvises within structure.]

DAY 1 — JUN 8 (record on arrival)

Location: Hotel Dubrovnik Palace exterior, or the ferry / port if coming by water. The geography is part of the message.

Opening: 'Dubrovnik. The week vertical drama goes European.'

Framing beat: 'I'm here with Maria Rua Aguete — she runs media and entertainment research for Omdia. She has the global market sizing. I have the distribution receipts. Together we're going to answer a question the European market keeps asking.' (Don't answer it yet.)

Field observation: whatever lands. Authentic > clever.

Close: 'Panel is [day]. I'll take you in.'

DAY 2 / PANEL DAY — JUN 9 or 10

Pre-panel (shoot outside room, 3 min before): '30 seconds. This is what I'm going to open with.' Tim delivers his opening line to camera, then cuts. No teleprompter, no notes.

Post-panel (same day): 'Three things that happened in that room.' One observation from Maria, one from Tim, one from the audience that surprised him.

DAY 3 / FINAL DAY — JUN 11

Wrap: 'Four days. Here's what changed my mind about the European market.' One specific thing. End: 'Next stop: APOS Bali, June 16.'

[CAPTION TEMPLATE — NEM version]
Day [N] — NEM Dubrovnik. Today: [what happened]. With [named people if cleared]. [Observation]. Day [N+1] tomorrow. / or: Next: Bali.

[POSTING CADENCE]
Evening posts, 7–9pm local (CET) — hits APAC morning LinkedIn sweet spot. Stuart edits, Tim films.
rationale: Beat sheet format (not script) — Tim improvises, which is more native. Day 1 deliberately withholds the panel question answer as a hook for Day 2 viewership. Pre-panel 30-sec is the most repostable format at conferences. NEM is under-tapped for Tim's European MIPCOM positioning — building a presence here now makes MIPCOM October easier. This is Category 21 (visual / travel vlog) and Category 11 (conference) combined.

title: Trifecta piece-to-camera — 'Netflix. Amazon. Disney. Now what?'
hook: Netflix. Amazon. Disney. All vertical. Now what?
coreArgument: The three largest US streaming platforms launching vertical feeds in the same fortnight is not a format experiment — it is an infrastructure acknowledgment that supersedes Peacock as the single biggest category validation to date.
whyNow: Netflix Clips launched April 30; Amazon Prime Video Clips launched May 8; Disney+ Locker Diaries launched February. The trifecta is confirmed this week. Tim lands in LA in 6 days. This piece-to-camera sets his authority before his first meeting and gives journalists something to reference when they Google him.
sourceEvidence: Netflix Clips: Variety Apr 30 2026 https://variety.com/2026/digital/news/netflix-new-mobile-app-vertical-video-feed-clips-1236734258/ | Amazon Prime Video Clips: Advanced Television May 11 2026 https://www.advanced-television.com/2026/05/11/prime-video-rolling-out-clips-vertical-feed/ | Disney+ Locker Diaries: Deadline Feb 2026 https://deadline.com/2026/02/disney-locker-diaries-vertical-drama-zombies-characters-1236724682/ Date checked: 2026-05-26.
timPOV: This is not Netflix experimenting with vertical. Netflix, Amazon, and Disney all telling their product teams 'build for portrait mode' in the same month — that is a supply-chain decision. What they cannot build from scratch is the catalogue and the production infrastructure. That is what COL spent three years building.
colRelevance: Every LA studio meeting on Jun 1-4 is now set against this backdrop. Every Microdrama-in-a-Box B2B conversation now has three proof points from the world's largest streamers. The Belloni pitch letter leads with this.
supportingPoints: **Open (0–3 sec):** Tim direct to camera, outdoors with depth behind him. 'Netflix. Amazon. Disney.' Pause. 'All vertical.' Hold. Do not rush.

**Beat 1 (3–20 sec):** 'In the last month, Netflix launched a vertical feed. Then Amazon. Disney has been running vertical since February. The three biggest streaming companies in the world just told their product teams: build for portrait mode.' Crisp, no adjectives.

**Beat 2 (20–40 sec):** 'That's not an experiment. An experiment is one streamer trying it. Three is a supply-chain decision.' Pause. 'The question now isn't whether vertical drama belongs in streaming. The question is who has the catalogue and the infrastructure to supply it.'
**Beat 3 (40–55 sec):** 'I'm in LA next week. The meetings just got a lot more interesting.' One beat. 'Guess what we're talking about.'
**Close (55–62 sec):** Cut. No sign-off.

**Caption (~90 words):** Netflix. Amazon. Disney. All launched vertical video feeds in the last 30 days. Three is not a coincidence — it is infrastructure. Netflix Clips is live in 9 markets. Amazon Clips launched May 8. Disney has been running vertical since February. The question is not whether vertical drama belongs in streaming anymore. The question is who builds the supply chain that connects these platforms to the content. I am in LA next week. The conversations just changed. Who should I be talking to?
risk: Sounding self-congratulatory. Frame the significance for the audience ('what this means for every studio in LA'), not for COL. Do not name COL in the video itself — let the profile do that. Avoid 'I told you so' tone; 'operator who saw this coming' is the register.
body:
[SHOOT INSTRUCTIONS]
Location: Outdoors, depth behind Tim — hotel terrace, street, FlareFlow studio entrance. NOT a desk, NOT a logo wall.
Wardrobe: Clean shirt, no jacket. Same register as the pre-LA piece.
Phone vertical, 3–4 takes, pick the best.
Audio: phone mic. Quiet outdoor space preferred.
Shoot Saturday or Sunday May 30-31. Post before Tim boards for LA.

[ON-CAMERA SCRIPT — ~60 seconds]

Netflix.

Amazon.

Disney.

(Beat. Don't rush it.)

All vertical.

(Hold.)

In the last month, Netflix launched a vertical feed. Then Amazon. Disney's been running vertical since February. The three biggest streaming companies in the world just told their product teams: build for portrait mode.

That's not an experiment. An experiment is one streamer trying it.

Three is a supply-chain decision.

(Pause.)

The question now isn't whether vertical drama belongs in streaming.

The question is who has the catalogue and the infrastructure to supply it at scale.

I'm in LA next week. The meetings just got a lot more interesting.

Guess what we're talking about.

[CAPTION TEXT]

Netflix. Amazon. Disney.

All launched vertical video feeds in the last 30 days.

Three isn't a coincidence — it's infrastructure.

Netflix Clips: live in 9 markets since April 30.
Amazon Prime Clips: launched May 8.
Disney+: vertical since February.

The question isn't whether vertical drama belongs in streaming anymore.

The question is who builds the supply chain that connects these platforms to the content they need.

I'm in LA next week. The conversations just changed.

Who should I be talking to?
rationale: Hook: three nouns, three seconds — the strongest possible opener because no setup needed. 'Three is a supply-chain decision' is Tim's sharpest framing of the validation moment — operator insight, not cheerleading. Forward close ties to LA trip without pitching it. Caption 'Who should I be talking to?' generates inbound from LA contacts. Outdoor, phone, no jacket — native to the format Tim is arguing for. This supersedes the Peacock piece in urgency but Tim can still shoot both: Peacock piece (the original prediction validated) + Trifecta piece (the industry-wide confirmation). If only one gets shot this weekend, it is this one.

title: The luxury tier just arrived in vertical — Marc Jacobs, P&G, and the full brand spectrum
hook: P&G is making soap operas. Marc Jacobs is making Met Gala comedies.
coreArgument: The brand-entry list for vertical content now spans luxury fashion (Marc Jacobs), FMCG (P&G, Unilever), and lifestyle (Crocs, Shopee, Starbucks, Maybelline) — the full consumer brand spectrum, confirming vertical as a legitimate media buy across every budget tier.
whyNow: Marc Jacobs 'The Scene' launched April 2026. P&G 'The Golden Pear Affair' documented May 2026. The full seven-brand list is now public. Tim's LA trip (Jun 1-4) includes brand CMO conversations — this post sets his authority in the brand-vertical intersection before those meetings. Also feeds the NEM Dubrovnik panel context.
sourceEvidence: Marc Jacobs 'The Scene': Marketing Dive April 2026 https://www.marketingdive.com/news/marc-jacobs-puts-luxury-spin-on-microdrama-content-rachel-sennott/818411/ | P&G 'The Golden Pear Affair': Marketing Dive May 2026 https://www.marketingdive.com/news/pg-native-to-debut-50-part-microdrama-series-as-genre-takes-off/807512/ | Starbucks, Maybelline, Crocs, Shopee: Marketing Brew 2026. Date checked: 2026-05-26.
timPOV: For two years I said brands are coming to vertical. I did not expect them to arrive from this many directions at once. P&G went back to its soap-opera roots, vertical screen. Marc Jacobs hired Rachel Sennott and made a scripted Met Gala comedy to sell a bag. Same format, completely different creative muscle. The question is not whether brands invest in vertical — it is who understands how to produce and distribute at scale across the entire spectrum.
colRelevance: Positions COL's Microdrama-in-a-Box infrastructure as the solution for brands operating across this documented spectrum. Sets up LA brand CMO meetings with a clear market thesis. Update B2B deck with the seven-brand list and the FMCG → luxury framing.
supportingPoints: P&G launched 'The Golden Pear Affair' — 50-episode scripted vertical series for the Native personal care brand, produced with Dentsu Entertainment. P&G's soap-opera heritage, vertical screen.
Marc Jacobs launched 'The Scene' — scripted microdrama starring and written by Rachel Sennott, debuting The Scene Bag. First in an ongoing series. Luxury brand, Hollywood writer.
Confirmed brand list as of May 2026: P&G, Unilever, Crocs, Shopee, Starbucks, Maybelline, Marc Jacobs. FMCG + lifestyle + luxury = full consumer spectrum.
The question has changed. Not 'will brands invest in vertical?' but 'who knows how to produce and distribute at scale across this spectrum?' Infrastructure question.
This is what we have been building for.
risk: Sounding like a sales pitch. Keep Tim as market observer who happens to be building the infrastructure — the COL reference ('this is what we have been building for') is the only product moment and it lands quiet. No COL mention by name in the body.
body:
P&G is making soap operas again.

Marc Jacobs is making Met Gala comedies.

For two years I've been saying brands are coming to vertical. I just didn't expect them to arrive from this many directions at once.

P&G: 'The Golden Pear Affair' — 50 episodes, scripted vertical series for the Native personal care brand, built with Dentsu Entertainment. Bringing soap-opera heritage to the phone screen.

Marc Jacobs: 'The Scene' — a scripted comedy starring Rachel Sennott, written to sell a bag. Hollywood writer, luxury brand, vertical format.

Same format. Completely different creative muscle.

The confirmed list now: P&G, Unilever, Crocs, Shopee, Starbucks, Maybelline, Marc Jacobs.

FMCG. Lifestyle. Luxury. The full consumer spectrum is in this format.

The question isn't whether brands will invest in vertical anymore. It's who understands how to produce and distribute at scale — across the whole spectrum from P&G to Marc Jacobs.

This is what we've been building for.
rationale: Opens with two sharp, specific images — Tim's trademark. Receipts > rhetoric: seven named brands, four product names. 'Same format. Completely different creative muscle.' is the operator insight that proves Tim understands both ends of the spectrum. Closes quietly with the COL implication. One idea per sentence. No hashtags. The post lands best Wednesday or Thursday of the LA trip week when Tim is in brand CMO meetings — posting it live in LA is the strongest version.

title: NEM Dubrovnik pre-panel piece-to-camera — '13 days. One question.'
coreArgument: A 60-second pre-panel piece-to-camera recorded the week before NEM builds European media-market anticipation before Tim arrives, gives trade press a B-roll quote, and sets up the NEM daily vlog series.
whyNow: NEM Dubrovnik June 8-11 is 13 days out. Panel topic: 'Microdrama: risky business or industry's next guilty pleasure?' With the Netflix/Amazon/Disney trifecta now confirmed, Tim's panel answer is sharper than when the panel was originally booked. Shoot Thursday-Friday May 28-29 so it's live over the June 1-4 LA trip and hits European audiences on Monday June 1.
sourceEvidence: Confirmed speaking engagement: data/state/speaking.json (seed-nem-dubrovnik-new-europe-market). Panel co-panellists: Maria Rua Aguete (Omdia), Cassandra Yang (RisingJoy), Lukasz Wysocki. Trifecta: intelligence item int_trifecta_vertical_2605. knowledge/VOICE.md on-camera rules. Date checked: 2026-05-26.
timPOV: The panel question answers itself this week. 'Risky business or guilty pleasure?' — neither. Netflix, Amazon, and Disney all launched vertical video feeds this month. That is infrastructure, not a guilty pleasure. I will explain what that means for the European market in Dubrovnik.
colRelevance: Builds Tim's European media-market profile before NEM — the corridor for CEE/Eastern European distribution conversations, an under-tapped market for COL's 1,700+ title catalogue. Pre-panel visibility drives more valuable conversations at the event.
supportingPoints: **Open (0–4 sec):** Tim direct to camera, outdoors with natural light. 'Thirteen days. Dubrovnik. One question.' Beat.

**Beat 1 (4–20 sec):** 'The panel question is: is microdrama risky business or the industry's next guilty pleasure?' Pause. 'This week Netflix, Amazon, and Disney all launched vertical video feeds. Does that sound risky to you?'

**Beat 2 (20–40 sec):** 'I am on stage with Maria Rua Aguete — she runs media and entertainment research at Omdia. Between her data and my receipts from the field, we are going to answer a question the European market has been circling for two years.' Hold.
**Beat 3 (40–55 sec):** 'If you are in Dubrovnik, I'll see you there. If you're not — I'll bring you in.' Forward setup for the NEM daily vlog.
**Close (55–62 sec):** 'June 8.' One beat. Cut. No sign-off.

**Caption (~70 words):** NEM Dubrovnik June 8-11. Panel question: Microdrama — risky business or the industry's next guilty pleasure? This week Netflix, Amazon, and Disney all launched vertical video feeds. That is not a guilty pleasure — that is infrastructure. Maria Rua Aguete (Omdia) and I are on stage with the data and the receipts. See you in Dubrovnik. Or I'll bring you in from the room.
risk: Risk 1: Sounding like conference promotion. Mitigation: the hook is the rhetorical move ('That's not risky — Netflix just built it'), not 'join me at NEM.' Risk 2: Maria Rua Aguete should know Tim is naming her before she sees it — send a DM or WhatsApp before posting. She will appreciate the advance notice.
body:
[SHOOT INSTRUCTIONS]
Location: Outdoors with natural light and depth. Singapore daylight, street or terrace, NOT a desk.
Wardrobe: Clean shirt, no jacket.
Phone vertical, 3–4 attempts, pick best take.
Audio: phone mic. Outdoors OK.
Shoot Thursday or Friday May 28-29. Post Friday May 29 evening (hits European Monday morning).

[ON-CAMERA SCRIPT — ~60 seconds]

Thirteen days. Dubrovnik. One question.

(Beat.)

The panel question is: is microdrama risky business — or just the industry's next guilty pleasure?

(Slight pause, then the punchline.)

This week, Netflix, Amazon, and Disney all launched vertical video feeds.

Does that sound risky to you?

(Hold. Don't explain it. Let the audience sit with it for two seconds.)

I'm on stage with Maria Rua Aguete — she runs media and entertainment research at Omdia. Between her data and my receipts from the field, we're going to answer a question the European market has been circling for two years.

If you're in Dubrovnik, I'll see you there.

If you're not — I'll bring you in.

June 8.

[CAPTION TEXT]

NEM Dubrovnik. June 8-11.

'Microdrama: risky business or the industry's next guilty pleasure?'

This week, Netflix launched a vertical feed. Then Amazon. Disney has been running vertical since February.

That is not a guilty pleasure. That is infrastructure.

Maria Rua Aguete (Omdia) and I are on stage in Dubrovnik to answer the question the European market keeps asking.

See you there — or I'll bring you in from the room.
rationale: Hook in 5 words — passes VOICE.md test. The rhetorical structure (state the panel question → answer it with the trifecta) is confident without arrogance. Naming Maria Rua Aguete builds the 'Tim knows the people who have the data' credibility. 'I'll bring you in from the room' is the hook for the NEM vlog series — audience investment before Tim lands. Posts Friday before LA trip: live during LA, hits European media Monday morning. Outdoor, phone, no jacket — native to the category Tim represents.
